etymology.description: 歹(歹) (dǎi, "skeletal remains") + 刀(刀) (dāo, "knife") = to tear apart -> line up
etymology.interpretation: The character 列 originally combines 歹(歹) (dǎi, "skeletal remains") with 刀(刀) (dāo, "knife") to convey the idea of cutting or tearing apart, akin to separating bones. This visual of arranging bones in a specific order or line leads to the modern meaning of 'line up.'

examples.chinese: 我们需要把桌椅排成一列(我們需要把桌椅排成一列)。
examples.pinyin: Wǒmen xūyào bǎ zhuōyǐ páichéng yī liè.
examples.english: We need to line up the tables and chairs.

examples.chinese: 士兵们整齐地列成一列(士兵們整齊地列成一列)，等待长官检阅(等待長官檢閱)。
examples.pinyin: Shìbīngmen zhěngqí de liè chéng yī liè, děngdài zhǎngguān jiǎnyuè.
examples.english: The soldiers lined up neatly, waiting for the officer's inspection.

examples.chinese: 火车一列接一列地驶过(火車一列接一列地駛過)。
examples.pinyin: Huǒchē yī liè jiē yī liè de shǐguò.
examples.english: The trains passed one line after another.
